Good evening, welcome to The Smith. My name is Ryan, I will be your server tonight. Can I start you off with anything to drink?
Thank you. Two sparkling waters, please. And could you tell us about the daily special?
Of course. Tonight it is a pan-seared salmon. But if I were you, I would go with our house specialty, the slow-cooked short rib.
That sounds perfect. One question first: my guest cannot eat nuts. Is there any allergen in the short rib?
No nuts at all, and if there were, the kitchen would prepare it separately. Would you like a salad with that?
Yes, one salad, with the dressing on the side, please. And we will split the check at the end.
Absolutely. And since you came before seven, your first drinks are on the house. If you cannot finish the short rib, we are happy to box up your leftovers.
Wonderful. If every restaurant were this easy, I would eat out every night. Thank you, Ryan!
If I were you, I would go with the special.
Could I have the dressing on the side?
We would like to split the check, please.
If they had it, I would order it.
Could I have the dressing on the side, please?
Is there any allergen in this dish?
Could we get a box for the leftovers?

Good evening, and welcome. Let me tell you about our specials tonight. Our daily special is a pan-seared salmon with a lemon butter sauce, served with roasted vegetables and a small salad. The house specialty, the one people come back for, is our slow-cooked short rib. It is on the menu every day, and honestly, it is what I would order myself. If you have any food allergies, just let me know and the kitchen will take care of it. Oh, and happy hour runs until seven, so your first drink is on the house. Take your time, and I will be right back to take your order.

Here is a simple way to talk about imaginary situations at a restaurant. We call it the second conditional. You take if plus a past-tense verb, and then would plus the base verb. For example, if you were not sure what to order, you would ask the server. Notice that we use were for everybody: if I were you, if she were here. This is different from the first conditional, which is for real, likely things: if they have the salmon, I will order it. Past plus would means it is imaginary. Present plus will means it is real. Get that difference, and you can give advice about any meal.
